description.en: Master Django models, field types, migrations, relationships, and ORM queries to interact with your database.
description.ta: Django மாதிரிகள், புல வகைகள், இடம்பெயர்வுகள், உறவுகள் மற்றும் ORM வினவல்களை தரவுத்தளத்துடன் தொடர்பு கொள்ள கற்றுக்கொள்ளுங்கள்.

story_variants.en:
## The Blueprint Room

Vaathiyaar unrolls a blueprint. "Before you build a house, you need a blueprint. In Django, **models** are your blueprints — they define the shape of your data. Django then builds the database tables for you automatically."

### What is a Model?
- A Python class that maps to a database table
- Each attribute = a column in the table
- Each instance = a row in the table
- Django creates SQL from your Python code

### Field Types

| Field | Purpose | SQL Equivalent |
|-------|---------|---------------|
| `CharField` | Short text | VARCHAR |
| `TextField` | Long text | TEXT |
| `IntegerField` | Whole numbers | INTEGER |
| `FloatField` | Decimals | REAL |
| `BooleanField` | True/False | BOOLEAN |
| `DateTimeField` | Date + time | DATETIME |
| `ForeignKey` | Many-to-one link | FOREIGN KEY |
| `ManyToManyField` | Many-to-many | Junction table |

### Migrations: Version Control for Databases
```bash
python manage.py makemigrations  # Detect changes
python manage.py migrate         # Apply changes
python manage.py showmigrations  # List all migrations
```

### ORM Queries
```python
Post.objects.all()                    # SELECT * FROM post
Post.objects.filter(author='alice')   # WHERE author='alice'
Post.objects.get(pk=1)                # WHERE id=1 (single)
Post.objects.create(title='Hello')    # INSERT INTO post
Post.objects.count()                  # SELECT COUNT(*)
```

> **Key Concept:** The Django ORM is an abstraction layer — you write Python, Django writes SQL. You can switch databases (SQLite → PostgreSQL) without changing your model code.

explanation.en: Import Django's models module — the foundation of the ORM

steps.line:
class Author(models.Model):
    name = models.CharField(max_length=100)
    email = models.EmailField(unique=True)
    bio = models.TextField(blank=True)

explanation.en: Define an Author model — Django auto-creates an 'id' primary key
__str__ controls how the object displays in admin and shell
Meta class configures model behavior — here fixing the plural name

steps.line:
class Post(models.Model):
    title = models.CharField(max_length=200)
    content = models.TextField()
    published = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)

explanation.en: auto_now_add sets the timestamp automatically when created
ForeignKey creates a many-to-one relationship — one author, many posts
ManyToMany — a post can have many categories, a category can have many posts
Posts start as drafts — default=False means unpublished by default

steps.line:
# In the Django shell:
# $ python manage.py shell

explanation.en: Open the Django interactive shell to test queries
Create and save a new author in one step

steps.line: post = Post.objects.create(title='Learn Django', content='Models are powerful!', author=author)

explanation.en: Create a post linked to the author via ForeignKey
Access all posts by this author using the related_name 'posts'
Double underscore traverses relationships — filter posts by author name
Chain filters: get published posts, newest first, limit to 5

output: Apply all migrations: admin, auth, blog, contenttypes, sessions

instruction.en: Create Django models for a library system: a 'Book' model with title, isbn, pages, and published_date fields, and an 'Author' model with name and birth_date. Link them with a ForeignKey. Write ORM queries to create an author, create two books, and filter books with more than 300 pages.
instruction.ta: நூலக அமைப்புக்கான Django மாதிரிகளை உருவாக்குங்கள்: title, isbn, pages, published_date புலங்களுடன் 'Book' மாதிரி, மற்றும் name, birth_date புலங்களுடன் 'Author' மாதிரி.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
from django.db import models

class Author(models.Model):
    name = models.CharField(max_length=100)
    birth_date = models.DateField(null=True, blank=True)

    def __str__(self):
        return self.name

class Book(models.Model):
    title = models.CharField(max_length=200)
    isbn = models.CharField(max_length=13, unique=True)
    pages = models.IntegerField()
    published_date = models.DateField()
    author = models.ForeignKey(Author, on_delete=models.CASCADE, related_name='books')

    def __str__(self):
        return self.title

# ORM Queries (in Django shell):
# author = Author.objects.create(name='Guido van Rossum')
# Book.objects.create(title='Python Intro', isbn='1234567890123', pages=250, published_date='2024-01-01', author=author)
# Book.objects.create(title='Advanced Python', isbn='1234567890124', pages=450, published_date='2024-06-01', author=author)
# thick_books = Book.objects.filter(pages__gt=300)
# print(thick_books)  # <QuerySet [<Book: Advanced Python>]>

en: Use models.ForeignKey to link Book to Author
objects.create() creates and saves in one step
Detects model changes and creates migration files
ta: மாதிரி மாற்றங்களைக் கண்டறிந்து இடம்பெயர்வு கோப்புகளை உருவாக்குகிறது

q.en: What does on_delete=models.CASCADE mean in a ForeignKey?

en: When the parent is deleted, all related objects are also deleted
ta: பெற்றோர் நீக்கப்படும்போது, தொடர்புடைய அனைத்து பொருள்களும் நீக்கப்படும்

q.en: How do you filter Django objects where a field is greater than a value?
q.ta: ஒரு புலம் ஒரு மதிப்பை விட அதிகமாக இருக்கும் Django பொருள்களை எவ்வாறு வடிகட்டுவது?
